title: Chapter 18: The Negotiation
content: "I'm going to Vantage."

Cass went still. Not the careful stillness of someone choosing their words — the absolute, biological stillness of a predator that had just heard a threat. He stood in the kitchen doorway of Aline's apartment, one hand braced against the frame, the other hanging at his side like he'd forgotten it existed. Morning light cut across his face, illuminating the scar tissue along his jaw, the hollows beneath his cheekbones. Two years underground had carved him into something gaunt and angular, but the look he gave her now was pure Cass — fierce, protective, and utterly unwilling to yield.
"No."
"That wasn't a question."

"Aline." He stepped forward. His voice dropped into that register she'd come to recognize: not command, not quite plea, something raw and dangerous in between. "He knows we're connected now. Walking into his office is walking into a trap."
She pulled on her jacket — the black one with the silver buttons, the one that made her shoulders look wider, the one that made her feel like armor instead of flesh. "Then it's a trap I choose."
"You don't know what he's capable of."

"I know exactly what he's capable of. You told me. You showed me the scars." She grabbed her keys from the bowl by the door. The metal was warm from yesterday's sunlight, or maybe that was just her blood running hot. "He destroyed you. He erased you. He took your work and your name and your life, and he did it smiling, and he's still out there, still smiling, still taking. And you want me to sit in this apartment and wait for you to handle it from a basement?"
"From a server room. From a network. From channels he can't—"

"From hiding, Cass." The word landed between them like a slap. She saw it hit — the flinch behind his eyes, the tightening of his jaw. Good. He needed to hear it. "I'm not your server to protect. And I'm not going to hide while you fight my battles underground."

She paused at the door. Her hand rested on the knob, and for a moment the silence between them was so complete she could hear his breathing — shallow, controlled, the breathing of a man who'd taught himself not to make sound. Two years of silence. Two years of shadows. She would not become another shadow he protected.
"I have the earpiece," she said, quieter. "You'll hear everything. You'll be there."
His voice came out rough, like gravel dragged across concrete. "If he touches you—"
"Then you'll hear it. And you'll know I chose it." She opened the door. "I'll be back."

Vantage Tower rose forty-three stories above the financial district, a blade of black glass and brushed steel that seemed to cut the sky itself. Aline had been here before — the label showcase, six months ago, when she'd been just another aspiring artist hoping for a moment of attention. She remembered the lobby's marble floors, the way her heels had clicked too loud against them, the way the receptionist had looked through her like she was furniture that could talk.

Now the receptionist recognized her. Now the elevator opened without a keycard, escorted by a security guard who said nothing and touched nothing, and Aline rode the forty-three floors alone with the humming of the cables and the cold clarity of her own pulse.
For Cass, she thought. You're doing this for Cass. And for yourself. And for every artist Marcus Webb has ever swallowed whole.

The elevator opened onto the executive floor. Same carpet — gray, thick, swallowing footsteps. Same hallway lined with abstract art. Same smell: old money and new cruelty, the sandalwood diffusers masking something more chemical, something that stung the sinuses if you breathed too deep. She didn't breathe too deep. She walked.
Marcus's office door was already open.

He was standing at the window — forty-three floors of vertical authority, the whole city spread beneath him like an offering. He turned when she entered, and the smile he wore was the same one he'd given her at the showcase: warm, measured, the smile of a man who had practiced friendliness until it became indistinguishable from the real thing.
"Aline. You look well."
"You look exactly the same, Marcus."
He gestured to the chair across from his desk. "Please. Sit."

She sat. The leather of his guest chair was butter-soft, the kind of material that cost more than most people's rent and gave nothing back — it absorbed her weight without resistance, without comfort, without any acknowledgment that she was there at all. Like the building. Like the man. She rested her hands on the armrests and let her eyes wander.

The art on the walls. She'd noticed it before, in passing — the abstract pieces, the mixed-media installations, the way they transformed a corporate office into something almost approaching a gallery. But now she looked. Really looked. The piece to her left: fractured geometry in rust and midnight blue, the pigment applied in thick, desperate layers, like someone had tried to paint their way out of a cage. She knew that style. She'd studied that style. She'd fallen in love with a whole album because of the cover art that used that exact technique, those exact colors, that exact fracturing.

Cass's second album. *Subterranean*. The cover had been painted by an artist named Kira Voss, who'd vanished from the art world three years ago — the same year Vantage Records acquired her catalog for a sum that was never disclosed.
Marcus collected his victims' work. Not just their music. Their art. The visible evidence of their souls, mounted on his walls like trophies.
She looked back at him, and this time, she was not nervous.
Marcus sat down across from her, steepled his fingers, and got straight to it. No pleasantries. No preamble. The mask of warmth dropped away like a curtain falling at the end of a performance.

"I know Cassian Mercer is alive. I know he's the ghost producer behind your music. I know he's been living underground for two years." A beat. His eyes — pale gray, almost silver, the color of scalpel steel — held hers without blinking. "I also know he's been surveilling you since before you ever met."
He slid a file across the desk. Manila. Ordinary. The kind of folder that held contracts and invoices and the mundane machinery of corporate life. But the contents were anything but mundane.
Aline opened it.

Screenshots. Dozens of them. Grainy, green-tinted, the unmistakable texture of night-vision camera feeds. Her apartment. Her bedroom. Her bathroom mirror, half-fogged from the shower, her reflection a ghost inside a ghost. Her sleeping — mouth slack, hair spread across the pillow, one hand curled beneath her chin. Her singing — standing at the kitchen counter, headphones on, eyes closed, lips shaping words she'd never meant anyone to hear. Her crying — seated on the bathroom floor, knees drawn to her chest, face buried in her hands, shoulders heaving with something that looked like it might never stop.
The paper was cold in her hands. Static-cold, printer-cold, the kind of cold that came from machines that processed human pain into data.

She studied each image. Took her time. Let the silence stretch until it was Marcus who shifted first — a barely perceptible adjustment of his shoulders, tiny, but she caught it. He'd expected her to gasp. To recoil. To let the horror show on her face like weather across a landscape.
She closed the file. Set it neatly on the desk between them.
"I know."

Marcus's smile flickered. It was barely a movement — a twitch at the corner of his mouth, a fractional narrowing of those pale eyes — but Aline saw it. The first crack in the composure of a man who had never, in his professional life, been surprised by a woman sitting across his desk.
"You KNOW he's been watching you sleep, and you're still with him?"
She leaned forward. The leather whispered beneath her. His cologne reached her — cedar and black pepper and something underneath, something animal, something that smelled like the inside of a trap.

"I know everything." She let the words land one at a time, each one a nail driven into the frame of his assumption. "The surveillance. The microphones. The GPS in the butterflies." His expression didn't change, but his fingers — still steepled — pressed tighter together. "I know he was inside my apartment. I know he's obsessive and controlling and deeply, profoundly damaged."
She paused. The clock on his desk ticked. The city hummed forty-three floors below.
"And I know YOU made him that way."

The silence that followed was absolute. Not the performative silence of a negotiation tactic — genuine, volcanic silence, the kind that filled the space between tectonic plates before the earthquake. Marcus's mask slipped. Just for a second, just for a single unguarded breath, she saw what lived beneath it: rage. Not the cold, calculated displeasure of a businessman thwarted. Real rage — hot and ancient and personal, the kind of fury that had been building for years, decades, a lifetime of grudges stacked like cordwood behind those beautiful, icy eyes.

Then he recovered. The mask slid back into place. But Aline had seen behind it now, and what she'd seen wasn't a master strategist. It was a man who hurt people because people had once hurt him, and who'd built an empire to ensure he'd never be the vulnerable one again.
"This isn't about the past." His voice was smooth again, polished, the vocal equivalent of the marble floors downstairs. "This is about the future. I'm offering you a deal."
He pushed the file aside — the photographs of her sleeping, her crying, her most private moments reduced to a prop he could discard — and folded his hands on the desk.

"Sign with Vantage. Publicly credit the label for your production. All of it — the sound design, the arrangements, the identity you've built. It becomes a Vantage product. A Vantage story." He smiled. That practiced smile. "And I'll bury what I know about Cass. The surveillance footage disappears. His existence stays a secret. He continues his little life in the dark, and you continue your career in the light."
"And if I don't?"

"Then I release the surveillance footage. All of it." He leaned back in his chair. It creaked — the only sound in the room, the leather protesting his weight, or maybe the man inside the leather protesting the shape he was about to take. "The world sees what your 'protector' really is. A stalker. A predator. A man who installed cameras in a woman's bedroom and watched her sleep and cry and sing, and called it love." His smile widened. "And you become famous, Aline. Just not for the reasons you wanted."
She looked at him. She looked at the file. She looked at the painting on the wall — Kira Voss's desperate geometry, rust and midnight blue, a soul trying to paint its way free — and then she stood.

Slowly. Deliberately. She buttoned her jacket, one silver button, two, and when she looked at Marcus again, she looked at him the way she'd learned to look at stage lights: head on, no flinching, let the brightness burn whatever it wanted to burn.
"You want to destroy him, I can't stop you."
A micro-expression of satisfaction. He thought she was surrendering.
"But I will never credit Vantage for what he built. Not one note. Not one beat. Not one second of silence."
His satisfaction curdled. "Then—"
"And if you release that footage—" She stepped around the chair. Walked toward the door. Stopped. Turned. "—you'll be proving to the world that you're the one who was watching through his cameras."
The silence that hit the room was different this time. Thicker. Sharper. Like the air before a lightning strike, every molecule charged and waiting.
"Because how else would you have gotten it?"

She watched it happen. Watched the calculation run behind his eyes — the rapid assembly of lies, the search for a plausible answer, the realization that there wasn't one. Marcus Webb was many things: ruthless, patient, intelligent. But he was also a man who had built his power on the assumption that the people he destroyed would stay destroyed, and the people he threatened would stay frightened. He had not accounted for Aline Rivers.

"Tell me, Marcus." She took one step back toward his desk. Just one. "How did you find his server feeds? Because those were air-gapped. No external access. No cloud backup. No internet-connected anything. I know — I've seen the setup." Another step. "The only person who could have accessed those feeds..."

His face went rigid. Not the controlled stillness of a negotiator managing his tells — the rigid, bloodless stillness of a man caught in a trap of his own design. Because she was right, and he knew she was right, and she knew he knew, and there was no lie in the world that could untangle the logic of it. You couldn't hack what wasn't connected. You couldn't steal what was physically unreachable. The evidence in his file was damning, all right — but not just of Cass.
She smiled. It was not a nice smile. It was the smile of a woman who had walked into a predator's office and found the one weapon the predator hadn't thought to guard.
"You didn't hack in," she said. "Someone inside his system gave them to you."
She turned. Walked to the door. Her heels struck the carpet — each step audible now, deliberate, the sound of a woman who no longer needed to be quiet.
She didn't look back.

The elevator doors closed. The box dropped — forty-three floors of controlled freefall, the cables humming their mechanical hymn, the floor numbers ticking downward like a countdown. Aline's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against her thighs and willed them to stop.
Her earpiece crackled.
"Aline." Cass's voice. Hoarse. Raw. Like he'd been holding his breath for the entire meeting and had only now remembered how to exhale. "You were—"
"Save it. Not now."

"She's right." Not to her — he was talking to someone else. His voice shifted, sharpened, took on the clipped precision of the man who'd built an underground network with his bare hands and a keyboard. "VERA — run a diagnostic. Full sweep. Who accessed the surveillance feeds in the last ninety days?"
A pause. The elevator passed the thirtieth floor. The twenty-fifth. The cables hummed. Aline's heartbeat filled the silence like a drum in an empty cathedral.
VERA's voice: cool, synthetic, measured. "Diagnostic running. Access logs deleted — timestamp indicates intentional removal approximately six weeks ago."
"Can you recover them?"

"Recovery in progress. Preliminary scan complete." Another pause. Longer this time. "Access logs deleted, but residual trace indicates—" The AI hesitated. If a machine could hesitate. "—internal access. The breach originated from within the network. Authorized credentials were used."
"Credentialed by whom?"
"Unable to determine. Log deletion was thorough. However, residual trace confirms: access pattern consistent with an active member of the network."
The elevator reached the lobby. The doors opened. Marble floors, sandalwood air, the receptionist who looked through everyone and everything.
Aline stepped out. Her legs were steady. Her hands had stopped shaking.

"One of us," Cass said. His voice was barely a whisper now — not the whisper of the phantom in the dark, but the whisper of a man who had just learned, for the second time in his life, that the knife in his back had been held by someone he trusted.

Someone inside his network. Someone who had access to his servers, his feeds, his secrets. Someone who had walked those underground corridors and sat at those underground terminals and chosen — actively, deliberately, with full knowledge of what it would cost — to hand Cassian Mercer's life to the man who had already destroyed it once.

Aline pushed through the tower's glass doors and stepped into the cold June sunlight. The city roared around her — traffic and voices and the relentless machinery of a world that didn't know or care that a war was being fought in its basements and boardrooms.
Her earpiece was quiet. Cass was quiet. VERA was quiet.

But somewhere in the underground, someone was not quiet. Someone was watching. Someone was waiting. Someone who had already betrayed Cass once and gotten away with it — and who was, right now, listening to the same silence, reading the same diagnostic, calculating their next move.
Aline walked. The wind bit at her cheeks. The city moved around her, indifferent, infinite, full of strangers who could be anyone.
One of us.
The words echoed in her skull like a gunshot in an empty room, and she could not stop them.
